title: Ypsilanti, Michigan

infobox.value: ([/ˌɪpsəˈlænti/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*IP-sə-LAN-tee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[4]](#cite_note-4)), commonly shortened to **Ypsi** ([/ˈɪpsiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*IP-see*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), is a city and [college town](/wiki/College_town "College town") located on the [Huron River](/wiki/Huron_River "Huron River") in [Washtenaw County, Michigan](/wiki/Washtenaw_County,_Michigan "Washtenaw County, Michigan"), United States. As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the city's population was 20,648. The city is bounded to the north by [Superior Charter Township](/wiki/Superior_Township,_Washtenaw_County,_Michigan "Superior Township, Washtenaw County, Michigan") and on the west, south, and east by [Ypsilanti Charter Township](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Township,_Michigan "Ypsilanti Township, Michigan") (a separately governed municipality). Ypsilanti is a part of the [Ann Arbor–Ypsilanti metropolitan area](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), the [Huron River Valley](/wiki/Huron_River "Huron River"), and the [Detroit–Warren–Ann Arbor combined statistical area](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit").

**Ypsilanti** ([/ˌɪpsəˈlænti/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*IP-sə-LAN-tee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[4]](#cite_note-4)), commonly shortened to **Ypsi** ([/ˈɪpsiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*IP-see*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), is a city and [college town](/wiki/College_town "College town") located on the [Huron River](/wiki/Huron_River "Huron River") in [Washtenaw County, Michigan](/wiki/Washtenaw_County,_Michigan "Washtenaw County, Michigan"), United States. As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the city's population was 20,648. The city is bounded to the north by [Superior Charter Township](/wiki/Superior_Township,_Washtenaw_County,_Michigan "Superior Township, Washtenaw County, Michigan") and on the west, south, and east by [Ypsilanti Charter Township](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Township,_Michigan "Ypsilanti Township, Michigan") (a separately governed municipality). Ypsilanti is a part of the [Ann Arbor–Ypsilanti metropolitan area](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), the [Huron River Valley](/wiki/Huron_River "Huron River"), and the [Detroit–Warren–Ann Arbor combined statistical area](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit").

Ypsilanti is known for being the home of [Eastern Michigan University](/wiki/Eastern_Michigan_University "Eastern Michigan University") (formerly the Michigan State Normal College) since the university's founding as Michigan's first [normal school](/wiki/Normal_school "Normal school") (teachers' college) in 1849, its location on the historic Detroit–Chicago Road (now [US Highway 12](/wiki/US_Highway_12 "US Highway 12")), its historic [Depot Town](/wiki/Depot_Town "Depot Town") commercial district, and for its distinctive [Ypsilanti Water Tower](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Water_Tower "Ypsilanti Water Tower") constructed in 1890. The city is closely associated with [Ford Motor Company's](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company") [Willow Run Bomber Plant](/wiki/Willow_Run_Airport "Willow Run Airport") which manufactured over 8,500 [Consolidated B-24 Liberator](/wiki/Consolidated_B-24_Liberator "Consolidated B-24 Liberator") heavy bombers during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"); Ypsilanti's [Ford Lake](/wiki/Ford_Lake "Ford Lake") was created when Ford Motor Company dammed the Huron River to generate hydroelectric power. Ypsilanti is also home to the first [Domino's Pizza](/wiki/Domino%27s "Domino's") location, located near EMU's [McKenny Student Union](/wiki/McKenny_Hall "McKenny Hall") in South Campus.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Originally a trading post established in 1809 by a [French-Canadian](/wiki/French-Canadian "French-Canadian") fur trader from [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), a permanent settlement was established on the east side of the [Huron River](/wiki/Huron_River_(Michigan) "Huron River (Michigan)") in 1823 by Major Thomas Woodruff. It was incorporated into the [Territory of Michigan](/wiki/Territory_of_Michigan "Territory of Michigan") as the village **Woodruff's Grove**. A separate community a short distance away on the west side of the river was established in 1825 under the name "Ypsilanti", after [Demetrios Ypsilantis](/wiki/Demetrios_Ypsilantis "Demetrios Ypsilantis"), a hero in the [Greek War of Independence](/wiki/Greek_War_of_Independence "Greek War of Independence").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Woodruff's Grove changed its name to Ypsilanti in 1829, the year its namesake effectively won the war for Greek Independence at the [Battle of Petra](/wiki/Battle_of_Petra "Battle of Petra"), with the two communities eventually merging. A bust of Demetrios Ypsilantis by Greek sculptor Christopher Nastos[[6]](#cite_note-6) stands between a Greek and a US flag at the base of the landmark [Ypsilanti Water Tower](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Water_Tower "Ypsilanti Water Tower").

[Elijah McCoy](/wiki/Elijah_McCoy "Elijah McCoy") lived in Ypsilanti with his family after moving from Canada following the Civil War. In Ypsilanti, racial barriers prevented him from working immediately as an engineer, so he took a position as a fireman and oiler for the [Michigan Central Railroad](/wiki/Michigan_Central_Railroad "Michigan Central Railroad") while experimenting with machinery in a home workshop.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was during this period in Ypsilanti that he developed his first automatic lubricator.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Today, Ypsilanti honors McCoy’s connection to the city with a Michigan [historical marker](/wiki/Historical_marker "Historical marker") at or near his former home and workshop.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In the late 19th century, Ypsilanti became known for producing full-body [long underwear](/wiki/Long_underwear "Long underwear") called [union suits](/wiki/Union_suit "Union suit"), commonly referred to as “Ypsilanti suits.” The garments were made at the Hay & Todd woolen mill, later renamed the Ypsilanti Underwear Company, along the Huron River near Forest Avenue. The suits were displayed at the [1893](/wiki/1893 "1893") [The World's Columbian Exposition](/wiki/The_World%27s_Columbian_Exposition "The World's Columbian Exposition"), (also known as the Chicago World's Fair) in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and distributed internationally, earning the city a reputation for quality garment production.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The factory employed many workers, particularly women, and featured a large mural of a union suit visible to passing trains.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The company declined in the early 20th century, was sold to the Oak Knitting Company in 1907, and the mill buildings were demolished in 1933.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Surviving examples of \*Ypsilanti\* underwear are preserved at the Ypsilanti Historical Museum.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Ypsilanti has played an important role in the automobile industry. From 1920 to 1922, Apex Motors produced the "ACE" car. The quarter mile stretch of land along [Michigan Avenue](/wiki/U.S._Route_12_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 12 in Michigan") became a hub for car dealerships starting in 1912 when the first car dealership opened, and ending in the 1990s when the last dealership closed. This area has been referred to as "The Amazing Quarter Mile" and was eventually added to the [Ypsilanti Historic District](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Historic_District "Ypsilanti Historic District"). This site is part of a collection of parcels known as the [Water Street Redevelopment Area](/wiki/Water_Street_Redevelopment_Area "Water Street Redevelopment Area"). It was in Ypsilanti that [Preston Tucker](/wiki/Preston_Tucker "Preston Tucker") (whose family owned the Ypsilanti Machine Tool Company) designed and built the prototypes for his [Tucker '48](/wiki/1948_Tucker_Sedan "1948 Tucker Sedan"). Tucker's story was related in the film *[Tucker: The Man and His Dream](/wiki/Tucker:_The_Man_and_His_Dream "Tucker: The Man and His Dream")*, directed by [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola").

In 1945, [Henry J. Kaiser](/wiki/Henry_J._Kaiser "Henry J. Kaiser") and [Joseph W. Frazer](/wiki/Joseph_W._Frazer "Joseph W. Frazer") bought the nearby [Willow Run](/wiki/Willow_Run "Willow Run") [B-24 Liberator](/wiki/B-24_Liberator "B-24 Liberator") bomber plant from [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), and started to make [Kaiser and Frazer](/wiki/Kaiser-Frazer "Kaiser-Frazer") model cars in 1947. The last [Kaiser](/wiki/Kaiser_Motors "Kaiser Motors") car made in Ypsilanti rolled off the assembly line in 1953, when the company merged with [Willys–Overland](/wiki/Willys "Willys") and moved production to [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"). [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors_Corporation "General Motors Corporation") purchased the Kaiser Frazer plant, and converted it into its [Hydramatic Division](/wiki/Hydramatic "Hydramatic") (now called its Powertrain division), beginning production in November 1953. The GM Powertrain Division ceased production at this facility in 2010.

Ypsilanti is also the location of the last [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_Motor_Car_Company "Hudson Motor Car Company") automobile dealership. Today, the former dealership is the site of the [Ypsilanti Automotive Heritage Collection](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Automotive_Heritage_Collection "Ypsilanti Automotive Heritage Collection"). The museum is the home to an original [Fabulous Hudson Hornet](/wiki/Fabulous_Hudson_Hornet "Fabulous Hudson Hornet") race car, which inspired the character Doc Hudson in the 2006 [Pixar](/wiki/Pixar "Pixar") animated film *[Cars](/wiki/Cars_(film) "Cars (film)")*.

In the early 1970s, the citizens reduced the penalty for the use and sale of [marijuana](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") to $5 (the Ypsilanti Marijuana Initiative; see also the [Human Rights Party](/wiki/Human_Rights_Party_(United_States) "Human Rights Party (United States)")).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1979, [Faz Husain](/wiki/Faz_Husain "Faz Husain") was elected to the Ypsilanti city council, the first [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") and the first native of India to win elected office in Michigan.

In the 1990s, Ypsilanti became the first city in Michigan to pass a [living wage](/wiki/Living_wage "Living wage") ordinance.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

On July 23, 2007, Governor [Jennifer Granholm](/wiki/Jennifer_Granholm "Jennifer Granholm") announced that Ypsilanti, along with the cities of [Caro](/wiki/Caro,_Michigan "Caro, Michigan") and [Clio](/wiki/Clio,_Michigan "Clio, Michigan"), was chosen by the Michigan State Housing Development Authority (MSHDA) to take part in the Blueprints for Michigan's Downtowns program. The award provides for an economic development consultant to assist Ypsilanti in developing a growth and job creation strategy for the downtown area.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

On June 23, 2020, Mayor [Beth Bashert](/wiki/Beth_Bashert "Beth Bashert") resigned after a controversial comment she made about race during a [Zoom](/wiki/Zoom_(software) "Zoom (software)") meeting.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Timeline")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Ypsilanti_August_2013_08_%28Michigan_Firehouse_Museum%29.jpg/250px-Ypsilanti_August_2013_08_%28Michigan_Firehouse_Museum%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ypsilanti_August_2013_08_(Michigan_Firehouse_Museum).jpg)

[Michigan Firehouse Museum](/wiki/Michigan_Firehouse_Museum "Michigan Firehouse Museum")

* 1929 – Miller Motors Hudson opens, it eventually becomes the last Hudson dealership in the world[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* 1931 – McKenny Union opens as the first [student union](/wiki/Student_activity_center "Student activity center") on the campus of a [teachers' college](/wiki/Teachers%27_college "Teachers' college").[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* 1937 - American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") musician [Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole") married singer and dancer Nadine Robinson at a private residence located at 370 First Avenue. The couple, who had met while performing in the [musical revue](/wiki/Musical_revue "Musical revue") [Shuffle Along](/wiki/Shuffle_Along "Shuffle Along"), were married on January 29, 1937.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* 1939–mid‑1940s – [Phyllis Diller](/wiki/Phyllis_Diller "Phyllis Diller") lived at 215 ½ Oakwood Street in Ypsilanti while her husband worked at the Willow Run bomber plant. Locals recall her singing in the [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") Church choir and performing at community events. She also developed early comedic routines referencing neighborhood life, which she later incorporated into her professional comedy acts.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* 1967–1969 – Ypsilanti resident [John Norman Collins](/wiki/John_Norman_Collins "John Norman Collins") was linked to the [Michigan Murders](/wiki/Michigan_Murders "Michigan Murders"), a series of killings targeting female students at the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") and Eastern Michigan University. He was arrested in July 1969 and convicted of one murder on August 19, 1970.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* 1998 – The Michigan Firehouse Museum is established preserving a firehouse built in 1898.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 4.47 square miles (11.58 km2), of which 4.29 square miles (11.11 km2) is land and 0.18 square miles (0.47 km2) (4.02%) is water.[[1]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2020-1)

The [Huron River](/wiki/Huron_River_(Michigan) "Huron River (Michigan)") flows through Ypsilanti with [Ford Lake](/wiki/Ford_Lake_(Michigan) "Ford Lake (Michigan)") on the southern edge of the city. [Paint Creek](/wiki/Paint_Creek_(Washtenaw_County,_Michigan) "Paint Creek (Washtenaw County, Michigan)") also runs through the city. The [Border-to-Border Trail](/wiki/Border-to-Border_Trail "Border-to-Border Trail") runs through the city.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the census[[32]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-32) of 2010, there were 19,435 people, 8,026 households, and 2,880 families residing in the city. The population density was 4,488.5 inhabitants per square mile (1,733.0/km2). There were 9,271 housing units at an average density of 2,141.1 per square mile (826.7/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 61.5% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 29.2% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.6% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 3.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 1.1% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 4.3% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 3.9% of the population.

There were 8,026 households, of which 18.4% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 19.7% were married couples living together, 12.1% had a female householder with no husband present, 4.0% had a male householder with no wife present, and 64.1% were non-families. 42.9% of all households were made up of individuals, and 7.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.06 and the average family size was 2.92.

The median age in the city was 25 years. 14.1% of residents were under the age of 18; 35.8% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 25.3% were from 25 to 44; 16.6% were from 45 to 64; and 8.3% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 49.7% male and 50.3% female.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the census[[2]](#cite_note-GR2-2) of 2000, there were 22,362 people, 8,551 households, and 3,377 families residing in the city. The population density was 5,081.5 inhabitants per square mile (1,962.0/km2). There were 9,215 housing units at an average density of 2,094.0 per square mile (808.5/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 61.40% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 30.58% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.44% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 3.18% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.07% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 1.32% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 3.01% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 2.47% of the population. 13.6% were of German ancestry, 6.8% Irish, 6.4% English and 5.5% [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") heritage according to [Census 2000](/wiki/Census_2000 "Census 2000").

There were 8,551 households, out of which 19.2% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 23.0% were married couples living together, 13.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 60.5% were non-families. 40.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 6.2% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.15 and the average family size was 2.96.

In the city, 15.9% of the population was under the age of 18, 38.2% was from 18 to 24, 26.4% from 25 to 44, 12.4% from 45 to 64, and 7.0% was 65 years of age or older. The median age was 24 years. For every 100 females, there were 89.8 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 86.2 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $28,610, and the median income for a family was $40,793. Males had a median income of $30,328 versus $26,745 for females. The per capita income for the city was $16,692. About 16.9% of families and 25.8% of the population were below the poverty line, including 30.1% of those under age 18 and 15.3% of those age 65 or over.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

See also: [National Register of Historic Places listings in Washtenaw County, Michigan](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Washtenaw_County,_Michigan "National Register of Historic Places listings in Washtenaw County, Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Martha_Washington_Theatre_front_view.jpg/250px-Martha_Washington_Theatre_front_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martha_Washington_Theatre_front_view.jpg)

Martha Washington Theatre

The [Martha Washington Theatre](/wiki/Martha_Washington_Theatre "Martha Washington Theatre") opened in 1915. It was initially operated by Florence W. Signor, who was the only woman theatre operator in Michigan at the time. It was sold to [W. S. Butterfield Theatres](/wiki/W._S._Butterfield_Theatres "W. S. Butterfield Theatres") in 1925. It was converted to an adult theatre in 1971, then to a strip club in 1982.[[33]](#cite_note-33) It closed in March 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and was damaged in a fire three months later.[[34]](#cite_note-:3-34)

[Domino's Pizza](/wiki/Domino%27s_Pizza "Domino's Pizza") was founded in Ypsilanti in 1960 near the campus of Eastern Michigan University.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

By 1963, Clara Owens established the Ypsilanti Greek Theater in Ypsilanti, Michigan for the performance of Greek theater productions.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Summer festivals that occur in [Depot Town](/wiki/Depot_Town "Depot Town") include the annual [Ypsilanti Heritage Festival](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Heritage_Festival "Ypsilanti Heritage Festival"), [Michigan ElvisFest](/wiki/Michigan_ElvisFest "Michigan ElvisFest"), the Orphan Car Festival, the [Michigan Brewers Guild](/wiki/Michigan_Brewers_Guild "Michigan Brewers Guild") Summer Beer Festival, a Latino festival.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Riverside Arts Center, established in 1994, features a 115-seat [black box theater](/wiki/Black_box_theater "Black box theater") and art gallery.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Since 2013, Ypsilanti has participated in [First Fridays](/wiki/First_Friday_(public_event) "First Friday (public event)"), an arts and culture-based monthly event that features a self-guided tour of participating businesses highlighting local artists, food and drink.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Ypsi Pride was established in 2017.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Estabrook_Elementary_School%2C_Ypsilanti%2C_Michigan_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Estabrook_Elementary_School%2C_Ypsilanti%2C_Michigan_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Estabrook_Elementary_School,_Ypsilanti,_Michigan_-_panoramio.jpg)

Estabrook Elementary School in Ypsilanti

### K–12 education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: K–12 education")]

[Ypsilanti Community Schools](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Community_Schools "Ypsilanti Community Schools") serve residents of the city, as well as parts of [Ypsilanti Township](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Township,_Michigan "Ypsilanti Township, Michigan") and [Superior Township](/wiki/Superior_Township,_Washtenaw_County,_Michigan "Superior Township, Washtenaw County, Michigan"). [Ypsilanti Public Schools](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Public_Schools "Ypsilanti Public Schools") and [Willow Run Community Schools](/wiki/Willow_Run_Community_Schools "Willow Run Community Schools") merged to form a new, united district on July 1, 2013. [Charter schools](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") in the city include [Arbor Preparatory High School](/wiki/Arbor_Preparatory_High_School "Arbor Preparatory High School").

The [High/Scope Perry Preschool Study](/wiki/HighScope#Effectiveness_of_the_program "HighScope"), which studied the effects of preschool on the later lives of low income children, occurred in Ypsilanti.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Higher education")]

A college town, Ypsilanti is the location of [Eastern Michigan University](/wiki/Eastern_Michigan_University "Eastern Michigan University"), founded in 1849 as Michigan State Normal School. Eastern Michigan University had over 10,000 undergraduate and more than 2000 graduate students in 2025.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[Washtenaw Community College](/wiki/Washtenaw_Community_College "Washtenaw Community College") sponsors an off-site extension center in Ypsilanti.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Media")]

Ypsilanti is served by daily newspapers from Detroit. Ypsilanti once had its own daily newspaper, the *Ypsilanti Press*, but that paper closed June 28, 1994, after 90 years in business.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Upon closing, the Press sold its masthead, archives and subscriber list to *[The Ann Arbor News](/wiki/The_Ann_Arbor_News "The Ann Arbor News")*, which then began publishing an Ypsilanti edition. *The Ann Arbor News* ceased publication on July 23, 2009; it was replaced by a new Internet-based news operation, [AnnArbor.com](/wiki/AnnArbor.com "AnnArbor.com"), which also produces print editions on Thursdays and Sundays. A weekly newspaper, the *[Ypsilanti Courier](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Courier "Ypsilanti Courier")*, is published every Thursday by Heritage Media from their [Saline, Michigan](/wiki/Saline,_Michigan "Saline, Michigan") offices. The only newspaper currently operating in Ypsilanti is [Eastern Michigan University](/wiki/Eastern_Michigan_University "Eastern Michigan University")'s independent newspaper *[The Eastern Echo](/wiki/The_Eastern_Echo "The Eastern Echo")*.

Local radio stations include:

* [WEMU FM](/wiki/WEMU "WEMU") - public radio
* [WQBR](/wiki/WQBR_(AM) "WQBR (AM)") - EMU's student-run radio
* [WDEO](/wiki/WDEO_(AM) "WDEO (AM)") - religious radio
* [WSDS](/wiki/WSDS "WSDS") - Spanish-language
* [WAAM](/wiki/WAAM "WAAM") - talk and news

### Filming location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ypsilanti,_Michigan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Filming location")]

* The 2009 film *[Whip It](/wiki/Whip_It_(film) "Whip It (film)")* was partly filmed in Ypsilanti.[[56]](#cite_note-56)
* The 2010 film *[Stone](/wiki/Stone_(2010_film) "Stone (2010 film)")* was partially filmed at Emmanuel Lutheran Church.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

* The 2017 film *[Three Christs](/wiki/Three_Christs "Three Christs")* includes establishing shots of Michigan Avenue and the water tower.[[60]](#cite_note-60)
